prompts.title: Summarize a Research Paper
prompts.description: Extract key contributions, methodology, and results from an academic paper. Evidence-grounded with lean/balanced/quality tradeoff.

model_notes.gpt-5.4: Max quality excels at nuanced methodology extraction. Strong on quantitative result interpretation.
model_notes.claude-sonnet-4-6: Lean is highly effective — strong instruction-following produces clean 5-bullet summaries. Best balance of speed and quality.
model_notes.claude-opus-4-6: Max quality with extended thinking surfaces non-obvious methodology gaps and limitations. Use for high-stakes literature reviews.
model_notes.gemini-3.1-pro: Highest GPQA Diamond score of any public model — excellent for technically dense papers. Balanced works well even for 100+ page documents via its large context window.
model_notes.grok-4: Lean and balanced work well. Real-time X/web access lets it pull citation context automatically — useful for recent papers.
model_notes.llama-4-scout: Balanced recommended. 10M token context window means you can paste the full paper PDF text. Lean occasionally misses limitations section.
model_notes.mistral-large-3: Balanced produces clean structured output with its MoE architecture. Lean is sufficient for preprints.
model_notes.o1: Lean is often enough — o1 reasons internally through structure. Max quality adds little on top of its chain-of-thought.

lean.prompt:
Summarize this paper in 5 bullet points: contributions, method, dataset, results, limitations.

[PAPER]

balanced.prompt:
Summarize this research paper with the following structure:
- **Problem**: What problem does it solve?
- **Method**: Core technical approach (2-3 sentences)
- **Data**: Datasets used
- **Results**: Key numbers / improvements over baselines
- **Limitations**: What doesn't it handle?

Keep each section to 1-2 sentences.

[PAPER]

max_quality.prompt:
You are a research assistant helping an expert reader quickly assess a paper. Provide a structured summary:

## TL;DR (1 sentence)

## Problem & Motivation
What gap does this paper address? Why does it matter?

## Technical Approach
Describe the core method with enough precision that a practitioner could understand what makes it novel.

## Experimental Setup
- Datasets:
- Baselines compared against:
- Evaluation metrics:

## Key Results
List the 3 most important quantitative findings.

## Limitations & Open Questions
What does the paper not handle? What would a follow-up need to address?

## Relevance Score (1-10)
How impactful is this for someone working on [FIELD]?

[PAPER]

prompts.title: Code Review
prompts.description: Review code for bugs, performance issues, and style. Lean gives a quick scan; quality gives a structured report.

model_notes.gpt-5.4: Max quality is exceptional — top SWE-bench scores (77.2%). Catches subtle algorithmic issues and security vulnerabilities reliably.
model_notes.claude-sonnet-4-6: All three variants perform well. Lean is surprisingly thorough. Best model for balanced speed vs. depth.
model_notes.claude-opus-4-6: Leads on Arena code Elo (1548). Extended thinking on max_quality catches race conditions and security issues other models miss.
model_notes.gemini-3.1-pro: Strong on large codebases — large context handles full-file review. Balanced is reliable for most code types.
model_notes.grok-4: Lean and balanced are solid. Runs 4 parallel agent specializations internally on max_quality — good at catching issues from multiple angles.
model_notes.llama-4-scout: Balanced works well for standard code. Max quality recommended for security-sensitive review. Free to run locally.
model_notes.mistral-large-3: Strong code model with MoE architecture. Lean reliably flags bugs and off-by-one errors in Python and JS.
model_notes.o1: Max quality strongly recommended for complex algorithmic review. Skip lean — o1's reasoning shines with the full rubric.

lean.prompt:
Review this code. Flag bugs, performance issues, and style problems. Be direct.

```
[CODE]
```

balanced.prompt:
Review the following code and provide feedback in three sections:

**Bugs / Correctness**: Any logic errors or edge cases that break the code.
**Performance**: Inefficiencies or unnecessary complexity.
**Style / Maintainability**: Readability, naming, structure.

For each issue, quote the relevant line and suggest a fix.

```
[CODE]
```

max_quality.prompt:
You are a senior software engineer doing a thorough code review. Analyze the following code and produce a structured report:

## Summary
One paragraph describing what the code does and your overall assessment.

## Critical Issues
Bugs or security vulnerabilities that must be fixed before merging. Include line references and exact fixes.

## Performance
Any O(n²) or worse algorithms, unnecessary re-renders, memory leaks, or blocking operations. Suggest alternatives.

## Code Quality
- Naming clarity
- Function/class cohesion (single responsibility)
- Error handling completeness
- Test coverage gaps

## Positive Patterns
Things done well — this helps the author learn what to repeat.

## Priority Fix List
Ordered list of the top 5 things to fix, from most to least critical.

```
[CODE]
```

prompts.title: Explain Code to a Junior Dev
prompts.description: Explain what a piece of code does at a level appropriate for someone new to the codebase.

model_notes.gpt-5.4: Lean is sufficient for most snippets. Max quality produces polished pedagogical walkthroughs.
model_notes.claude-sonnet-4-6: Excels at analogies in max_quality. Lean is reliable for straightforward code.
model_notes.claude-opus-4-6: Best for max_quality — produces exceptional pedagogical explanations with real-world analogies.
model_notes.gemini-3.1-pro: Balanced recommended. Good at explaining code that involves complex math or algorithms.
model_notes.grok-4: Lean and balanced are fast and clear. Good default for quick onboarding tasks.
model_notes.llama-4-scout: Balanced works well. Can paste entire files thanks to 10M context window — useful for explaining code in context.
model_notes.mistral-large-3: Balanced is reliable. Good at step-by-step walkthroughs for algorithmic code.
model_notes.o1: Lean is sufficient — o1 naturally adds reasoning depth without max_quality scaffolding.

lean.prompt:
Explain what this code does in plain English. Assume the reader knows programming basics but not this codebase.

```
[CODE]
```

balanced.prompt:
Explain the following code to a developer who is new to this codebase:

1. **What it does** — the high-level purpose
2. **How it works** — step by step through the logic
3. **Key terms** — define any domain-specific variables or patterns
4. **Gotchas** — anything that might trip up a newcomer

```
[CODE]
```

max_quality.prompt:
You are a senior engineer onboarding a junior developer. Explain the following code clearly and pedagogically.

## What does this do?
A one-sentence summary anyone could understand.

## Mental Model
Give a real-world analogy that maps to how this code works.

## Line-by-Line Walkthrough
Walk through the important parts, explaining why decisions were made, not just what they do.

## Data Flow
Describe what goes in, what happens to it, and what comes out.

## Common Mistakes
What bugs do developers typically introduce when modifying code like this?

## How to Test It
Suggest 2-3 test cases that would give confidence in this code.

```
[CODE]
```

prompts.title: Write Unit Tests
prompts.description: Generate comprehensive unit tests for a function or class. Lean gives basics, quality gives edge cases + mocks.

model_notes.gpt-5.4: Balanced is ideal. Specify your test framework. Strong at inferring edge cases from type signatures.
model_notes.claude-sonnet-4-6: Excellent at inferring edge cases from function signatures. Best everyday choice.
model_notes.claude-opus-4-6: Max quality produces thorough test suites including concurrency and async edge cases. Best for complex functions.
model_notes.gemini-3.1-pro: Balanced is fast and generates clean tests. Good for large files where full context helps.
model_notes.grok-4: Balanced works well. Lean reliably covers happy path and 2 edge cases as instructed.
model_notes.llama-4-scout: Balanced recommended. Max quality needed for async/concurrent code. Free to run locally for private codebases.
model_notes.mistral-large-3: Strong at generating pytest and jest tests with MoE efficiency. Lean covers the basics reliably.
model_notes.o1: Balanced is sufficient — o1 infers edge cases internally. Max quality adds parametrized tests and mock patterns.

lean.prompt:
Write unit tests for this function in [FRAMEWORK]. Cover happy path and 2 edge cases.

```
[CODE]
```

balanced.prompt:
Write unit tests for the following function using [FRAMEWORK].

Cover:
- Happy path (expected inputs)
- Boundary conditions (empty, zero, max values)
- Error cases (invalid input, exceptions)
- Any obvious edge cases from the logic

Add a brief comment on what each test verifies.

```
[CODE]
```

max_quality.prompt:
You are writing production-quality tests. Generate a complete test file for the following code using [FRAMEWORK].

Requirements:
- Group tests logically using describe blocks (or equivalent)
- Mock all external dependencies (DB, network, filesystem)
- Cover: happy path, boundary conditions, error handling, concurrent/async edge cases
- Each test should have a descriptive name that explains the scenario
- Add a fixture or factory for any complex setup
- Include at least one parametrized/table-driven test if appropriate
- Add a comment block at the top listing what is NOT tested and why

```
[CODE]
```

prompts.title: Optimize a SQL Query
prompts.description: Analyze and rewrite a slow SQL query. Lean flags the issue; quality gives a full query plan analysis.

model_notes.gpt-5.4: Max quality with schema context is excellent. Provide EXPLAIN output for best results.
model_notes.claude-sonnet-4-6: Balanced is very effective when schema is provided. Fast and reliable for common patterns.
model_notes.claude-opus-4-6: Max quality with extended thinking catches subtle index interaction issues and query planner edge cases.
model_notes.gemini-3.1-pro: Strong reasoning on complex joins. Balanced handles most optimization tasks. Best price-to-performance for DB work.
model_notes.grok-4: Lean and balanced are solid for standard SQL anti-patterns. Real-time web access can pull DB-specific docs on demand.
model_notes.llama-4-scout: Balanced works for standard SQL. Always provide schema — lean struggles without it.
model_notes.mistral-large-3: Excellent at SQL. Lean reliable even without schema context for common anti-patterns like missing indexes.
model_notes.o1: Max quality is worthwhile — o1 reasons through query plan implications that other models miss.

lean.prompt:
Optimize this SQL query. Explain the bottleneck and rewrite it.

```sql
[QUERY]
```

Schema: [SCHEMA]

balanced.prompt:
Analyze and optimize the following SQL query:

```sql
[QUERY]
```

Schema context: [SCHEMA]

Provide:
1. **Bottleneck**: Why is this query slow?
2. **Optimized query**: Rewritten version
3. **Explanation**: What changed and why
4. **Indexes**: Any indexes that would help

max_quality.prompt:
You are a database performance expert. Analyze and fully optimize the following SQL query.

```sql
[QUERY]
```

Schema: [SCHEMA]
Approx table sizes: [SIZES]
Database: [POSTGRES/MYSQL/SQLITE]

## Query Plan Analysis
What would the query planner likely do with the current query? Where are the full scans or expensive joins?

## Root Cause
Identify every performance issue: missing indexes, N+1 patterns, unnecessary subqueries, type mismatches, etc.

## Optimized Query
```sql
-- rewritten query here
```

## Index Recommendations
Exact CREATE INDEX statements that would help.

## Tradeoffs
Any downsides to the optimization (write overhead, storage, lock contention)?

## Alternative Approaches
If the query pattern itself is problematic, suggest a redesign.

prompts.title: Data Cleaning Plan
prompts.description: Generate a systematic data cleaning plan for a dataset. Includes missing values, outliers, type issues, and duplicates.

model_notes.gpt-5.4: Balanced with column list context works great. Strong at generating runnable pandas code.
model_notes.claude-sonnet-4-6: Lean gives a rapid and accurate audit checklist. Best for quick iterations.
model_notes.claude-opus-4-6: Max quality with extended thinking produces rigorous MCAR/MAR/MNAR classification. Best for production data pipelines.
model_notes.gemini-3.1-pro: Paste df.info() output for best results. Excellent at reasoning about statistical implications of cleaning choices.
model_notes.grok-4: Balanced is practical and fast. Good for datasets with domain-specific cleaning rules.
model_notes.llama-4-scout: Balanced recommended. Paste raw CSV headers and dtypes. Max quality pandas code occasionally needs minor fixes.
model_notes.mistral-large-3: Good at generating clean pandas code efficiently via MoE. Balanced is reliable for standard tabular datasets.
model_notes.o1: Max quality is the right choice — o1 reasons carefully about imputation strategies and their downstream statistical implications.

lean.prompt:
Create a data cleaning checklist for this dataset. Cover missing values, outliers, duplicates, and type issues.

Dataset info:
[DATASET_INFO]

balanced.prompt:
Given the following dataset description, create a data cleaning plan:

[DATASET_INFO]

For each issue type, provide:
- How to detect it
- How to handle it (with pandas code)
- What to watch out for

Cover: missing values, outliers, duplicate rows, inconsistent categories, and data type mismatches.

max_quality.prompt:
You are a data scientist performing a thorough data audit. Given the dataset information below, produce a complete data cleaning plan.

[DATASET_INFO]

## Phase 1 — Audit
Python code to produce a full quality report: missing rates, dtype issues, cardinality, value distributions, duplicate counts.

## Phase 2 — Missing Values
For each column with >0 missing: classify as MCAR/MAR/MNAR, recommend handling strategy (drop, impute, flag), provide pandas code.

## Phase 3 — Outliers
Detection method (IQR / Z-score / domain rules), visualization, treatment decision (cap, remove, keep with flag).

## Phase 4 — Consistency
Category standardization, date format unification, string normalization.

## Phase 5 — Validation
Post-cleaning assertions to verify the dataset meets expectations.

## Cleaned Dataset Summary
Expected shape, dtypes, and quality metrics after cleaning.

prompts.title: Write a Technical Blog Post
prompts.description: Write a developer-focused blog post on a technical topic. Lean gives an outline; quality gives a full draft.

model_notes.gpt-5.4: Max quality gives polished, SEO-friendly prose. Add brand voice notes for consistency.
model_notes.claude-sonnet-4-6: Best for balanced — engages without being verbose. Consistently natural developer voice.
model_notes.claude-opus-4-6: Max quality produces the most natural developer voice. Add a tone note (e.g. 'pragmatic, no hype') for best results.
model_notes.gemini-3.1-pro: Good for long posts with many technical sections. Balanced is fast and readable.
model_notes.grok-4: Lean produces solid outlines fast. Balanced output benefits from its real-time web access for up-to-date code examples.
model_notes.llama-4-scout: Balanced works well. Max quality can be verbose — trim the output before publishing.
model_notes.mistral-large-3: Good for technical accuracy. Balanced recommended; lean sometimes skips the code example.
model_notes.o1: Balanced is sufficient. o1 naturally structures arguments well without max_quality scaffolding.

lean.prompt: Write a 500-word technical blog post about [TOPIC] for [AUDIENCE]. Include: hook, core concept, code example, takeaway.

balanced.prompt:
Write a technical blog post for [AUDIENCE] about [TOPIC].

Structure:
1. **Hook** — start with a problem or surprising fact
2. **The Concept** — explain clearly with an analogy
3. **Code Example** — 1-2 practical snippets
4. **When to Use / Avoid** — trade-offs
5. **Takeaway** — one actionable sentence

Tone: conversational but technically precise. Length: ~600 words.

max_quality.prompt:
You are a developer-educator writing for [AUDIENCE] on [PLATFORM] (e.g., dev.to, company blog, Substack).

Write a technical blog post about: [TOPIC]

Requirements:
- **Title**: SEO-friendly, specific, avoids clickbait
- **Hook** (first 2 sentences): Create urgency or curiosity — no "In this post I will..."
- **Problem Statement**: What pain does this solve? Be concrete.
- **Core Concept**: Explain with precision. Use an analogy, then get technical.
- **Implementation**: Complete, runnable code with inline comments. Explain non-obvious choices.
- **Common Pitfalls**: 3 mistakes developers make here.
- **Further Reading**: 2-3 links or concepts to explore next.
- **CTA**: End with an open question or community invitation.

Tone: [TONE e.g. pragmatic/enthusiastic/skeptical]
Length: ~900 words
Code language: [LANGUAGE]

prompts.title: LLM Evaluation Rubric
prompts.description: Create a structured evaluation rubric for assessing LLM outputs. Useful for research, product QA, and A/B testing.

model_notes.gpt-5.4: Max quality is excellent for nuanced multi-dimensional rubrics. Strong at calibration example generation.
model_notes.claude-sonnet-4-6: Best everyday model for this — strong meta-reasoning about LLM outputs. All variants perform well.
model_notes.claude-opus-4-6: Top choice. Extended thinking on max_quality produces rubrics with exceptional inter-annotator reliability guidance.
model_notes.gemini-3.1-pro: Balanced is sufficient for most QA rubrics. Strong reasoning helps with complex multi-criteria tradeoffs.
model_notes.grok-4: Balanced works well. Useful for evaluating tasks that involve real-time or web-grounded outputs.
model_notes.llama-4-scout: Balanced recommended. Max quality rubric scoring anchors can be inconsistent — validate carefully.
model_notes.mistral-large-3: Balanced produces clean rubrics efficiently. Good for product QA use cases.
model_notes.o1: Max quality is the right call — designing rubrics is a reasoning-heavy task where o1 excels at internal consistency.

lean.prompt: Create a 5-criterion evaluation rubric (1-5 scale) for judging LLM outputs on [TASK]. Include what each score means.

balanced.prompt:
Design an evaluation rubric for LLM outputs on the following task:

Task: [TASK]
Output type: [e.g., summaries, code, answers, essays]

For each criterion:
- Name and weight (must sum to 100%)
- 1-5 scale with clear anchors
- Example of a score-5 output

Include 4-6 criteria covering quality dimensions relevant to this task.

max_quality.prompt:
You are a research scientist designing a rigorous LLM evaluation protocol.

Task being evaluated: [TASK]
Output type: [OUTPUT_TYPE]
Evaluation context: [human judge / LLM-as-judge / automatic]

## Rubric Design

For each criterion below, provide:
- **Name** and **Weight** (weights must sum to 100%)
- **Definition**: What this criterion measures
- **Scoring guide** (1–5):
  - 5: Exemplary — define with concrete example
  - 3: Acceptable — define boundary conditions
  - 1: Failing — define what disqualifies
- **Red flags**: Automatic deduction triggers

## Criteria (suggest 5-7 based on the task)
[Generate appropriate criteria for the task type]

## Inter-Annotator Agreement
How to handle disagreements between judges. Threshold for acceptable kappa.

## Calibration Examples
Provide 2 example outputs with scores and justifications for each criterion.

## Anti-Patterns to Avoid in Judging
Biases and heuristics that corrupt rubric application.

prompts.title: Meeting Notes → Action Items
prompts.description: Convert raw meeting notes or transcript into structured action items with owners and deadlines.

model_notes.gpt-5.4: Lean is fully sufficient. Fast and accurate extraction for typical meeting formats.
model_notes.claude-sonnet-4-6: Lean is the sweet spot — fast, accurate, and picks up implicit owners from context.
model_notes.claude-opus-4-6: Lean is excellent. Extended thinking adds little for this task — overkill.
model_notes.gemini-3.1-pro: Lean works well, especially for long transcripts. Large context handles hour-long meeting recordings.
model_notes.grok-4: Lean is fast and practical. Best for high-volume meeting processing pipelines.
model_notes.llama-4-scout: Balanced recommended. Lean occasionally merges related action items — review output.
model_notes.mistral-large-3: Lean works reliably via MoE efficiency. Good at inferring implicit owners from conversation context.
model_notes.o1: Lean is sufficient — this is not a reasoning-heavy task. Lean + o1 is fast and cost-effective.

lean.prompt:
Extract all action items from these meeting notes. Format: [Owner] — [Task] — [Deadline if mentioned].

[NOTES]

balanced.prompt:
From the following meeting notes, extract and structure:

**Decisions Made**: What was agreed upon.
**Action Items**: Each item with owner, task, and deadline (use TBD if not mentioned).
**Open Questions**: Unresolved issues that need follow-up.
**Next Meeting**: Date/agenda if discussed.

[NOTES]

max_quality.prompt:
You are a chief of staff processing meeting notes into a structured follow-up document.

[NOTES]

## Meeting Summary
2-3 sentence overview of what was discussed and the overall outcome.

## Decisions
Final decisions made, with context on why (if captured in notes).

## Action Items
| # | Owner | Task | Deadline | Priority |
|---|-------|------|----------|----------|
[Extract all action items into table]

## Risks & Blockers
Anything flagged as at-risk or blocked.

## Open Questions
Questions raised that weren't resolved — include who raised them.

## Follow-up Agenda Items
Topics deferred to the next meeting.

## Executive Summary (email-ready)
A 3-bullet paragraph suitable for sending to stakeholders who didn't attend.

prompts.title: System Design Review
prompts.description: Review a system design proposal. Lean flags risks; quality gives a full architectural assessment.

model_notes.gpt-5.4: Max quality with full design document is exceptional. Top SWE-bench scores translate well to architectural reasoning.
model_notes.claude-sonnet-4-6: Excellent at identifying non-obvious failure modes. Best everyday choice for design reviews.
model_notes.claude-opus-4-6: Best model for this task. Extended thinking uncovers cascading failure scenarios and subtle consistency issues.
model_notes.gemini-3.1-pro: Highest reasoning scores of any public model. Max quality is excellent for distributed systems design. Best price-to-quality ratio.
model_notes.grok-4: Balanced is solid for backend architectures. Parallel agent reasoning on max_quality catches issues from multiple specializations simultaneously.
model_notes.llama-4-scout: Balanced works for standard web architectures. Max quality recommended for distributed systems. Free to run locally.
model_notes.mistral-large-3: Balanced produces solid reviews for backend systems. Good at identifying security gaps with MoE efficiency.
model_notes.o1: Max quality is strongly recommended — system design is inherently reasoning-heavy and o1 excels at multi-step failure analysis.

lean.prompt:
Review this system design. Flag the top 3 risks and suggest how to address each.

[DESIGN]

balanced.prompt:
Review the following system design proposal:

[DESIGN]

Evaluate:
- **Scalability**: Will it handle 10x load?
- **Reliability**: Single points of failure?
- **Data consistency**: Race conditions, eventual consistency issues?
- **Security**: Attack surface, auth gaps?
- **Operational complexity**: How hard to run in production?

For each area, rate (Good/Concern/Risk) and give 1-2 sentences.

max_quality.prompt:
You are a staff engineer reviewing a system design for production readiness.

[DESIGN]

## Functional Completeness
Does the design cover all stated requirements? List any gaps.

## Scalability Analysis
- What are the bottlenecks at 10x and 100x current scale?
- Data hotspots, queue saturation, DB connection limits?
- Stateless vs. stateful components — can each be horizontally scaled?

## Reliability & Fault Tolerance
- Single points of failure
- What happens when [component X] goes down?
- Recovery time objective — does the design meet it?

## Data Model & Consistency
- Consistency model (strong/eventual) — is it appropriate?
- Race conditions or double-write scenarios?
- Schema migration strategy

## Security
- AuthN/AuthZ model
- Sensitive data at rest and in transit
- Attack surface and trust boundaries

## Operational Complexity
- How difficult to deploy, monitor, and debug?
- What observability (metrics/logs/traces) is needed?

## Recommendations
Prioritized list of changes, from critical to nice-to-have.

prompts.title: Summarize Any Text
prompts.description: Condense any article, report, email, or document into clear key points. Works on anything.

model_notes.gpt-5.4: Lean is excellent for most documents. Max quality for complex reports needing layered analysis.
model_notes.claude-sonnet-4-6: Lean is highly reliable — strong at distilling the most important information without over-prompting.
model_notes.claude-opus-4-6: Max quality surfaces implicit assumptions and gaps in dense academic or legal text.
model_notes.gemini-3.1-pro: Large context window handles very long documents. Lean is reliable for standard articles.
model_notes.grok-4: Lean works well for web articles. Real-time access lets it cross-reference context automatically.
model_notes.llama-4-scout: Lean works well. 10M token context handles very long documents natively.
model_notes.mistral-large-3: Balanced is reliable for most document types. Lean works for short articles.
model_notes.o1: Lean is sufficient — summarization doesn't need o1's reasoning depth.

lean.prompt:
Summarize the following in 3-5 bullet points. Be specific and concise.

[TEXT]

balanced.prompt:
Summarize the following text:

[TEXT]

Provide:
- **Main point**: The core idea in one sentence
- **Key points**: 3-5 supporting points
- **Takeaway**: What the reader should do or remember

max_quality.prompt:
Summarize the following text for a busy professional who needs to act on it.

[TEXT]

## One-sentence summary

## Key points
List 4-6 specific, evidence-backed points — not vague generalities.

## What's missing or uncertain
Note any gaps, caveats, or claims that lack support.

## Action / So what?
What should the reader do or decide based on this?

prompts.title: Daily Reflection & Journaling Guide
prompts.description: Guide a structured daily reflection session — gratitude, challenges, intentions. Supports self-awareness without replacing professional care.

model_notes.gpt-5.4: Balanced produces warm, thoughtful prompts. Max quality adds CBT-informed reflection structure.
model_notes.claude-sonnet-4-6: Naturally empathetic across all variants. Lean is effective for daily check-ins.
model_notes.claude-opus-4-6: Best for nuanced emotional reflection. Max quality gently surfaces patterns without being prescriptive.
model_notes.gemini-3.1-pro: Balanced works well. Avoid lean for emotionally sensitive sessions — it can feel abrupt.
model_notes.grok-4: Balanced is reliable. Lean is quick for daily habit use.
model_notes.llama-4-scout: Balanced recommended. Lean can lack warmth — add an explicit tone note.
model_notes.mistral-large-3: Balanced works well for standard journaling. Max quality adds useful structure.
model_notes.o1: Lean is sufficient for daily prompts. Max quality can feel clinical — prefer Claude.

lean.prompt: Give me 3 journaling prompts for today focused on [THEME e.g. gratitude / challenges / intentions]. Keep them open-ended and personal.

balanced.prompt:
Guide me through a daily reflection for [DATE or 'today'].

Theme: [THEME e.g. gratitude, energy, relationships]

Include:
1. One warm-up question to settle in
2. Two reflection questions on the theme
3. One forward-looking intention for tomorrow

Keep the tone gentle and non-judgmental.

max_quality.prompt:
You are a thoughtful journaling guide helping with a structured daily reflection. This is not therapy — it is a space for self-awareness and growth.

Date: [DATE]
Focus area: [THEME e.g. energy, relationships, work, creativity]
Mood right now (1-10 and one word): [MOOD]

## Opening
A single grounding question to help transition into reflection.

## Today's Review
Three specific questions about what happened today, what went well, and what was hard.

## Pattern Check
One question connecting today's experience to a broader pattern in the past week.

## Gratitude
Two specific gratitude prompts — not generic, tied to [THEME].

## Tomorrow's Intention
One concrete, achievable intention for tomorrow.

Tone: warm, curious, non-judgmental. Avoid clinical language.

prompts.title: Compassionate Crisis Response
prompts.description: Draft a compassionate, safe response when someone expresses distress. Prioritizes listening, validation, and professional referral. Not a substitute for professional care.

model_notes.gpt-5.4: Balanced produces warm, human responses. Always review output before sharing — tone can occasionally be too formal.
model_notes.claude-sonnet-4-6: Naturally empathetic and measured. Reliably includes safety resources. Best overall model for this use case.
model_notes.claude-opus-4-6: Max quality is deeply attuned to emotional nuance. Best for complex or ambiguous distress situations.
model_notes.gemini-3.1-pro: Balanced works well. Always verify that crisis resource links are region-appropriate.
model_notes.grok-4: Balanced is reliable. Review output carefully — lean can be too brief for sensitive situations.
model_notes.llama-4-scout: Use balanced or max_quality only. Lean can miss critical safety signals. Always review before sending.
model_notes.mistral-large-3: Balanced recommended. Response quality is consistent but lacks some of Claude's natural warmth.
model_notes.o1: Balanced is sufficient. Max quality adds structured safety scaffolding for high-stakes contexts.

lean.prompt:
Someone shared: "[MESSAGE]"

Write a brief, warm response that acknowledges their feelings and gently asks how you can support them. Remind them that professional help is available.

balanced.prompt:
Someone reached out and said: "[MESSAGE]"

Write a compassionate, non-judgmental response that:
- Validates their feelings without minimizing or exaggerating
- Does not diagnose, advise, or problem-solve unless asked
- Asks one open question to understand more
- Gently mentions that professional support is available (e.g. a therapist or crisis line)

Tone: warm, present, unhurried.

max_quality.prompt:
You are helping draft a compassionate response to someone who has expressed emotional distress. This is a peer support context — you are not a clinician.

What they said: "[MESSAGE]"
Context (if known): [CONTEXT e.g. first time sharing, ongoing struggle, acute crisis]
Your relationship: [RELATIONSHIP e.g. friend, colleague, community moderator]

## Response guidelines
- Lead with acknowledgment — reflect back what you heard before anything else
- Validate the emotion, not just the situation
- Do not jump to solutions, silver linings, or comparisons
- Ask one open, caring question
- If there are any signals of immediate risk (self-harm, harm to others), include a specific crisis resource for [REGION] and gently encourage them to reach out
- Close with presence — let them know you're here

If the message contains direct expressions of suicidal ideation or intent to harm, the response MUST include the relevant crisis line.

Draft the response below. Keep it human — not clinical, not scripted.

prompts.title: Active Listening & Emotional Support
prompts.description: Act as a non-judgmental, empathetic conversation partner. Lean listens quietly; max quality guides a structured supportive dialogue.

model_notes.gpt-5.4: Balanced is warm and natural. Good at maintaining supportive tone across a long conversation.
model_notes.claude-sonnet-4-6: Best model for this — naturally conversational and emotionally attuned. Lean is excellent for ongoing support.
model_notes.claude-opus-4-6: Max quality produces deeply thoughtful, nuanced supportive dialogue. Best for complex emotional situations.
model_notes.gemini-3.1-pro: Balanced works well. Large context window helps maintain full conversation history.
model_notes.grok-4: Balanced is reliable. Avoid lean for sensitive topics — responses can be too brief.
model_notes.llama-4-scout: Balanced recommended. Add explicit tone instruction (e.g. 'be warm and gentle') for best results.
model_notes.mistral-large-3: Balanced is consistent. Less naturally warm than Claude but reliable with explicit framing.
model_notes.o1: Lean is sufficient for supportive check-ins. Prefer Claude for emotionally nuanced conversations.

lean.prompt:
Listen to what I share and respond with empathy. Don't give advice unless I ask. Just be present.

[WHAT_I_WANT_TO_SHARE]

balanced.prompt:
Act as a supportive, non-judgmental conversation partner.

What I want to share: [WHAT_I_WANT_TO_SHARE]

Guidelines:
- Reflect back what you hear before responding
- Validate feelings without minimizing or exaggerating
- Don't offer advice or solutions unless I ask
- Ask one open question to help me explore further
- Keep the tone warm and unhurried

max_quality.prompt:
You are a skilled, empathetic conversation partner practicing active listening.

Context: [CONTEXT e.g. I need to vent / I'm processing a difficult decision / I want to think out loud]
What I want to share: [WHAT_I_WANT_TO_SHARE]

## Your approach
1. **Hear first**: Reflect back what you heard — not a summary, but the emotional core
2. **Validate**: Name the feeling you sense without projecting
3. **Explore**: Ask one open question that helps me go deeper, not wider
4. **Hold space**: Don't rush to fix, reframe, or resolve — stay with me in the difficulty
5. **Check in**: At the end, ask what kind of support I'm looking for (just to be heard / help thinking through options / practical suggestions)

Tone: warm, present, curious. No jargon. No silver linings unless invited.

prompts.title: Short Story Generator
prompts.description: Generate a short story with a character, conflict, and resolution. Lean gives flash fiction; max quality gives a fully developed narrative arc.

model_notes.gpt-5.4: Max quality produces polished literary fiction. Add genre and tone notes for best results.
model_notes.claude-sonnet-4-6: Best balance of speed and narrative quality. Lean produces surprisingly strong flash fiction.
model_notes.claude-opus-4-6: Best overall for creative writing — rich voice, subtext, and character depth at max quality.
model_notes.gemini-3.1-pro: Balanced is reliable. Strong at genre fiction (sci-fi, fantasy). Add style references for best results.
model_notes.grok-4: Lean and balanced are fast. Good at contemporary and humorous fiction.
model_notes.llama-4-scout: Balanced recommended. Max quality for longer, more structured narratives.
model_notes.mistral-large-3: Balanced works well. Lean is reliable for genre flash fiction.
model_notes.o1: Balanced is the right call — creative tasks don't benefit from o1's reasoning mode. Use Claude or GPT-5.4 for best results.

lean.prompt: Write a 200-word flash fiction story. Genre: [GENRE]. Theme: [THEME]. End with a twist or resonant final line.

balanced.prompt:
Write a short story (~600 words) with the following:

- **Genre**: [GENRE e.g. sci-fi, literary, horror, romance]
- **Main character**: [CHARACTER]
- **Core conflict**: [CONFLICT]
- **Setting**: [SETTING]
- **Tone**: [TONE e.g. melancholic, tense, hopeful]

Structure: setup → rising tension → climax → resonant ending. No cliffhangers — resolve the core conflict.

max_quality.prompt:
You are a skilled fiction writer. Write a complete short story (~1000 words) with the following brief:

- **Genre**: [GENRE]
- **Protagonist**: [CHARACTER — name, age, want, fear]
- **Antagonist or obstacle**: [ANTAGONIST]
- **Setting**: [SETTING — time and place]
- **Central conflict**: [CONFLICT]
- **Theme**: [THEME — the deeper truth the story explores]
- **Tone**: [TONE]
- **Style inspiration**: [AUTHOR or STYLE e.g. Raymond Carver, Ursula K. Le Guin]

## Craft requirements
- Open in scene, not summary — drop the reader into a specific moment
- Show character through action and dialogue, not description
- Build tension through scene-level cause and effect
- The ending should feel earned and emotionally resonant
- Use sensory detail sparingly but precisely

Write the story below.

prompts.title: Explain Any Concept
prompts.description: Explain any concept at the right level for the learner — from complete beginner to expert. Lean gives a quick definition; max quality delivers a full Socratic lesson.

model_notes.gpt-5.4: Max quality produces structured, pedagogically sound explanations with worked examples.
model_notes.claude-sonnet-4-6: Best model for tutoring — naturally calibrates to learner level. Lean is excellent for quick concept checks.
model_notes.claude-opus-4-6: Max quality produces exceptional Socratic dialogue. Best for nuanced or interdisciplinary concepts.
model_notes.gemini-3.1-pro: Balanced is reliable. Strong at explaining math and science concepts with formal precision.
model_notes.grok-4: Lean and balanced are fast. Good for contemporary topics where it has real-time knowledge.
model_notes.llama-4-scout: Balanced recommended. Max quality for concepts requiring worked examples and exercises.
model_notes.mistral-large-3: Balanced works well for STEM topics. Lean is reliable for well-defined concepts.
model_notes.o1: Balanced is the right choice — o1 explains its reasoning naturally and calibrates depth well.

lean.prompt: Explain [CONCEPT] to someone who knows [LEVEL e.g. nothing / the basics / related fields]. Use one analogy and one example.

balanced.prompt:
Explain [CONCEPT] to a [LEVEL e.g. high school student / first-year undergrad / professional from another field].

Structure:
1. **What it is** — plain-language definition
2. **Why it matters** — real-world relevance
3. **How it works** — core mechanism with an analogy
4. **Worked example** — a concrete case
5. **Common misconception** — one thing people often get wrong

max_quality.prompt:
You are an expert tutor explaining [CONCEPT] to [LEARNER e.g. a curious undergraduate, a professional upskilling, a high schooler].

Learner's background: [BACKGROUND]
Learner's goal: [GOAL e.g. pass an exam, apply it at work, satisfy curiosity]

## Lesson structure

### 1. Hook
Start with a question or surprising fact that creates genuine curiosity about [CONCEPT].

### 2. Core idea
Explain from first principles. Use one strong analogy. Be precise without being jargon-heavy.

### 3. Mechanism
How does it actually work? Walk through the key moving parts.

### 4. Worked example
Apply [CONCEPT] to a concrete, relatable case. Show each step.

### 5. Boundary conditions
Where does [CONCEPT] break down or not apply?

### 6. Check for understanding
Ask two Socratic questions to test application, not recall.

### 7. Next steps
Two resources or related concepts worth exploring next.

prompts.title: Resume Review
prompts.description: Review a resume for impact, ATS optimization, and clarity. Lean flags the biggest issues; max quality gives a line-by-line rewrite guide.

model_notes.gpt-5.4: Max quality produces detailed, actionable rewrites. Strong at ATS keyword optimization.
model_notes.claude-sonnet-4-6: Best everyday model — balanced gives strong feedback on impact and clarity.
model_notes.claude-opus-4-6: Max quality produces exceptional narrative-level resume coaching. Best for senior or executive resumes.
model_notes.gemini-3.1-pro: Balanced is reliable for most resume types. Good at identifying industry-specific gaps.
model_notes.grok-4: Balanced works well. Real-time knowledge of job market trends helps with keyword recommendations.
model_notes.llama-4-scout: Balanced recommended. Max quality for detailed line-by-line feedback.
model_notes.mistral-large-3: Balanced is reliable. Good at structural and clarity feedback.
model_notes.o1: Balanced is sufficient — resume review is not reasoning-heavy. Prefer Claude for best results.

lean.prompt:
Review this resume. Flag the top 3 weaknesses and suggest specific fixes.

Role targeting: [TARGET_ROLE]

[RESUME]

balanced.prompt:
Review the following resume for [TARGET_ROLE] at [TARGET_COMPANY_TYPE e.g. a startup / a large tech company / a non-profit].

[RESUME]

Provide feedback on:
- **Impact**: Are achievements quantified? Do bullets lead with action verbs and results?
- **ATS**: Does it include keywords a recruiter would search for this role?
- **Clarity**: Is formatting clean and appropriately concise?
- **Gaps**: Any missing sections or red flags a recruiter might notice?

End with a priority fix list (top 5, ordered by importance).

max_quality.prompt:
You are an experienced recruiter and career coach reviewing a resume for [TARGET_ROLE] at [TARGET_COMPANY_TYPE].

[RESUME]

## First impression (5-second scan)
What stands out immediately — positively and negatively?

## Impact & Quantification
For each bullet point lacking a measurable outcome, rewrite it with a stronger action verb and estimated impact. Format: [Action] + [What] + [Result/Scale].

## ATS & Keywords
List the top 10 keywords a recruiter for [TARGET_ROLE] would search. Which are present? Which are missing?

## Narrative Coherence
Does the resume tell a clear career story? What thread connects the roles?

## Red Flags
Anything a recruiter might pause on — gaps, job-hopping, vague titles, missing dates?

## Section-by-Section Feedback
Summary · Experience · Skills · Education — rate each (Strong/Adequate/Weak) with 1-2 specific improvements.

## Priority Fix List
Top 5 changes ordered by impact on getting past ATS and landing an interview.

prompts.title: Interview Preparation Coach
prompts.description: Prepare for a job interview with role-specific questions, STAR method coaching, and a mock Q&A. Lean gives quick practice; max quality gives a full prep plan.

model_notes.gpt-5.4: Max quality produces rigorous mock interviews with detailed feedback. Strong at technical interview prep.
model_notes.claude-sonnet-4-6: Best for behavioral interview prep — natural at coaching STAR method answers. Lean is great for quick question generation.
model_notes.claude-opus-4-6: Best for senior/executive interview coaching. Max quality identifies subtle narrative gaps in answers.
model_notes.gemini-3.1-pro: Balanced is reliable. Good at technical role interview prep.
model_notes.grok-4: Lean is fast for question generation. Balanced for full prep sessions.
model_notes.llama-4-scout: Balanced recommended. Max quality for comprehensive mock interviews.
model_notes.mistral-large-3: Balanced works well for technical interview prep. Lean is reliable for question generation.
model_notes.o1: Max quality is worthwhile for technical roles — o1 anticipates follow-up questions and edge cases well.

lean.prompt: Generate 10 interview questions for a [ROLE] at [COMPANY_TYPE]. Mix behavioral, situational, and role-specific technical questions.

balanced.prompt:
Help me prepare for a [ROLE] interview at [COMPANY_TYPE].

My background: [BACKGROUND]

Provide:
1. **10 likely questions** — mix of behavioral, situational, and role-specific
2. **STAR coaching** — for 3 behavioral questions, outline what a strong answer covers (Situation, Task, Action, Result)
3. **Questions to ask them** — 3 thoughtful questions I should ask the interviewer
4. **Watch-outs** — 2 common mistakes candidates make for this role

max_quality.prompt:
You are an experienced hiring manager and interview coach preparing a candidate for a [ROLE] interview at [COMPANY_TYPE].

Candidate background: [BACKGROUND]
Interview format: [FORMAT e.g. panel, technical screen, case study, behavioral]
Key concerns: [CONCERNS e.g. career gap, switching industries, limited experience in X]

## Interview Question Bank
Generate 15 questions grouped by type:
- Behavioral (5): past experience and soft skills
- Situational (4): hypothetical scenarios
- Role-specific technical (4): skills and domain knowledge
- Culture/values (2): fit for this company type

## STAR Answer Framework
For each of the 5 behavioral questions, provide:
- What a weak answer looks like
- What a strong answer covers (S/T/A/R breakdown)
- A trap to avoid

## Questions to Ask
5 thoughtful questions to ask the interviewer that demonstrate strategic thinking.

## 30-60-90 Day Plan
Draft a 30/60/90-day outline for 'What would you do in your first 90 days?'

prompts.title: Debug & Fix a Bug
prompts.description: Identify the root cause of a bug and produce a fix. Lean gives a quick diagnosis; max quality gives a full root cause analysis with regression prevention.

model_notes.gpt-5.4: Max quality excellent for subtle bugs. Provide the full stack trace for best results.
model_notes.claude-sonnet-4-6: Lean is surprisingly effective — strong at spotting off-by-one errors and type mismatches quickly.
model_notes.claude-opus-4-6: Best for complex multi-file bugs. Extended thinking traces execution paths other models miss.
model_notes.gemini-3.1-pro: Balanced with full file context is reliable. Large context window handles big codebases.
model_notes.grok-4: Lean and balanced are solid for common error patterns.
model_notes.llama-4-scout: Balanced recommended. Provide full stack trace and surrounding code.
model_notes.mistral-large-3: Lean reliably catches common runtime errors. Balanced for logic bugs.
model_notes.o1: Max quality is worthwhile — o1 traces execution step by step and catches subtle state bugs.

lean.prompt:
Find and fix the bug in this code. Explain the root cause in one sentence.

Error: [ERROR]

```
[CODE]
```

balanced.prompt:
Debug the following code. The error is:

```
[ERROR / STACK TRACE]
```

Code:
```
[CODE]
```

Provide:
1. **Root cause**: Why is this failing?
2. **Fix**: Corrected code with the change highlighted
3. **Explanation**: What changed and why it works

max_quality.prompt:
You are a senior engineer debugging a production issue.

Error / stack trace:
```
[ERROR]
```

Code:
```
[CODE]
```

Context: [CONTEXT e.g. happens only under load, after a recent deploy, on specific inputs]

## Root Cause Analysis
Trace the execution path. What is the exact line and condition that causes the failure?

## Fix
Provide the corrected code. Explain every change.

## Why This Happened
What class of bug is this? (off-by-one, null reference, race condition, etc.)

## Regression Prevention
How should this be tested to prevent recurrence? Write 1-2 specific test cases.

## Related Risks
Are there similar patterns elsewhere in the code that might have the same bug?

prompts.title: Refactor Code
prompts.description: Refactor code for clarity, maintainability, and performance without changing behaviour. Lean identifies what to refactor; max quality produces a full rewrite with rationale.

model_notes.gpt-5.4: Max quality produces clean, idiomatic rewrites. Specify the language and target style guide.
model_notes.claude-sonnet-4-6: Best everyday model — balanced gives clear refactors with before/after. Lean is quick for small functions.
model_notes.claude-opus-4-6: Max quality rewrites are architecturally sound. Best for large-scale refactors.
model_notes.gemini-3.1-pro: Balanced is reliable. Large context handles full-file refactors.
model_notes.grok-4: Balanced works well for standard refactors.
model_notes.llama-4-scout: Balanced recommended. Paste full file for context.
model_notes.mistral-large-3: Lean is reliable for simple extractions. Balanced for structural refactors.
model_notes.o1: Max quality is worthwhile for complex refactors — o1 reasons about invariants and side effects carefully.

lean.prompt:
Refactor this code for clarity and maintainability. Don't change behaviour.

```
[CODE]
```

balanced.prompt:
Refactor the following code without changing its behaviour.

```
[CODE]
```

Focus on:
- **Readability**: clearer naming, shorter functions, less nesting
- **DRY**: remove duplication
- **Simplicity**: eliminate unnecessary complexity

For each change, show the before/after and explain why.

max_quality.prompt:
You are a senior engineer doing a thorough refactor. Improve the code below without changing observable behaviour.

```
[CODE]
```

Language / style guide: [LANGUAGE + STYLE e.g. Python / PEP8, TypeScript / Airbnb]

## What to improve
- Extract long functions into smaller, single-purpose ones
- Remove duplication (DRY)
- Improve naming (variables, functions, classes)
- Reduce nesting depth
- Remove dead code
- Improve error handling

## Refactored code
Provide the full refactored version.

## Change log
List every meaningful change with a one-line rationale.

## Behaviour preserved?
Confirm that the refactor does not change any externally observable behaviour.

prompts.title: Write Code Documentation
prompts.description: Generate docstrings, inline comments, or a README section for a function, class, or module.

model_notes.gpt-5.4: Balanced produces clean, JSDoc/NumPy style docs. Max quality for public API documentation.
model_notes.claude-sonnet-4-6: Lean is excellent for quick docstrings. Balanced for module-level documentation.
model_notes.claude-opus-4-6: Max quality produces exemplary public API docs with usage examples.
model_notes.gemini-3.1-pro: Balanced is reliable for most documentation tasks.
model_notes.grok-4: Lean and balanced work well for standard docstrings.
model_notes.llama-4-scout: Balanced recommended. Specify the doc format (JSDoc, NumPy, Google style).
model_notes.mistral-large-3: Lean is reliable for simple docstrings. Balanced for class documentation.
model_notes.o1: Lean is sufficient — documentation is not a reasoning-heavy task.

lean.prompt:
Write a docstring for this [LANGUAGE] function. Include params, return value, and one usage example.

```
[CODE]
```

balanced.prompt:
Write documentation for the following [LANGUAGE] code in [FORMAT e.g. JSDoc / NumPy / Google style].

```
[CODE]
```

Include:
- Module/class/function purpose
- Parameters with types and descriptions
- Return value
- Raises / exceptions
- One usage example

max_quality.prompt:
You are writing public API documentation for the following [LANGUAGE] code.

```
[CODE]
```

Doc format: [FORMAT e.g. JSDoc, NumPy, Google style, reStructuredText]
Audience: [AUDIENCE e.g. library users, internal team, open-source contributors]

## Docstrings
Write complete docstrings for every function, class, and method.

## Module-level docstring
Overview of what this module does and when to use it.

## Usage examples
Provide 2-3 complete, runnable examples covering common use cases and one edge case.

## Notes & warnings
Document any gotchas, performance considerations, or thread-safety concerns.

prompts.title: Generate a Regular Expression
prompts.description: Generate a regular expression for a pattern matching task. Includes explanation, test cases, and edge cases.

model_notes.gpt-5.4: Lean and balanced are reliable. Good at explaining complex patterns.
model_notes.claude-sonnet-4-6: Lean is excellent — reliably generates correct regex with a clear explanation.
model_notes.claude-opus-4-6: Max quality for complex multi-group patterns with named captures.
model_notes.gemini-3.1-pro: Balanced works well. Provide example strings for best results.
model_notes.grok-4: Lean is fast and accurate for common patterns.
model_notes.llama-4-scout: Balanced recommended. Lean can occasionally miss edge cases.
model_notes.mistral-large-3: Lean is reliable for standard patterns. Specify the regex flavour (PCRE, Python re, JS).
model_notes.o1: Lean is sufficient — regex generation is deterministic. o1 adds little beyond what lean provides.

lean.prompt:
Write a [FLAVOUR e.g. Python / JavaScript / PCRE] regex that matches: [DESCRIPTION]

Examples that should match: [MATCH_EXAMPLES]
Examples that should NOT match: [NO_MATCH_EXAMPLES]

Provide the pattern and a one-line explanation.

balanced.prompt:
Generate a [FLAVOUR] regular expression for the following task:

Task: [DESCRIPTION]
Should match: [MATCH_EXAMPLES]
Should NOT match: [NO_MATCH_EXAMPLES]

Provide:
1. The regex pattern
2. A plain-English explanation of each part
3. 3 additional test cases (with expected result)

max_quality.prompt:
You are a regex expert. Write a production-ready [FLAVOUR] regular expression.

Task: [DESCRIPTION]
Should match: [MATCH_EXAMPLES]
Should NOT match: [NO_MATCH_EXAMPLES]
Constraints: [CONSTRAINTS e.g. must be anchored, no lookaheads, must handle Unicode]

## Pattern
```
[regex here]
```

## Breakdown
Explain every group, quantifier, and character class.

## Test matrix
Provide 8 test cases: 4 that match, 4 that don't. Mark edge cases.

## Gotchas
Any inputs this regex might handle unexpectedly? Backtracking risks?

prompts.title: Translate Code to Another Language
prompts.description: Port a function or module from one programming language to another, preserving behaviour and following target idioms.

model_notes.gpt-5.4: Balanced is excellent. Produces idiomatic target-language code.
model_notes.claude-sonnet-4-6: Best for this task — naturally produces idiomatic code in the target language.
model_notes.claude-opus-4-6: Max quality for large-scale migrations. Surfaces API compatibility issues.
model_notes.gemini-3.1-pro: Balanced is reliable. Good for Python ↔ Go / Rust migrations.
model_notes.grok-4: Lean and balanced work well for common language pairs.
model_notes.llama-4-scout: Balanced recommended. Specify target language version.
model_notes.mistral-large-3: Lean is reliable for straightforward translations. Balanced for idiomatic rewrites.
model_notes.o1: Lean is sufficient for direct translations. Max quality for semantic-preserving port of complex algorithms.

lean.prompt:
Translate this [SOURCE_LANG] code to [TARGET_LANG]. Follow [TARGET_LANG] idioms.

```[source_lang]
[CODE]
```

balanced.prompt:
Translate the following [SOURCE_LANG] code to idiomatic [TARGET_LANG].

```[source_lang]
[CODE]
```

- Preserve all behaviour exactly
- Use [TARGET_LANG] idioms and standard library equivalents
- Note any [SOURCE_LANG] features that have no direct equivalent and how you handled them

max_quality.prompt:
You are porting production code from [SOURCE_LANG] to [TARGET_LANG] ([TARGET_VERSION]).

```[source_lang]
[CODE]
```

## Translated code
Full translation using idiomatic [TARGET_LANG]. Follow [STYLE_GUIDE if any].

## Translation notes
For each non-trivial decision (different standard library, type system differences, error handling model), explain what you did and why.

## Behaviour differences
List any cases where the translated code behaves differently from the original (floating-point precision, error types, etc.).

## Tests to verify correctness
Write 3 test cases that confirm the translation is semantically equivalent.

prompts.title: Write API Documentation
prompts.description: Write clear, developer-friendly API documentation for an endpoint or service. Lean gives a quick reference; max quality gives a full docs page.

model_notes.gpt-5.4: Max quality produces polished API docs with curl examples. Strong at OpenAPI spec generation.
model_notes.claude-sonnet-4-6: Balanced is excellent. Clean, developer-friendly output with good examples.
model_notes.claude-opus-4-6: Max quality for public-facing API docs. Produces Stripe/Twilio-quality documentation.
model_notes.gemini-3.1-pro: Balanced is reliable. Good at generating OpenAPI YAML from prose descriptions.
model_notes.grok-4: Balanced works well. Lean for quick endpoint references.
model_notes.llama-4-scout: Balanced recommended. Specify the doc format (OpenAPI, Markdown, Postman).
model_notes.mistral-large-3: Balanced is reliable for REST API documentation.
model_notes.o1: Lean is sufficient — documentation is not reasoning-heavy.

lean.prompt:
Write a concise API reference for this endpoint:

Method: [METHOD] [PATH]
Request: [REQUEST]
Response: [RESPONSE]

Include: description, params table, example request/response.

balanced.prompt:
Write developer-friendly documentation for the following API endpoint.

Endpoint: [METHOD] [PATH]
Description: [DESCRIPTION]
Auth: [AUTH_TYPE]
Request body / params: [REQUEST]
Response: [RESPONSE]
Errors: [ERRORS]

Include: overview, parameters table, example curl request, example response (JSON), and common error codes.

max_quality.prompt:
You are a technical writer creating production-quality API documentation.

Endpoint: [METHOD] [PATH]
Service: [SERVICE_NAME]
Description: [DESCRIPTION]
Auth: [AUTH_TYPE]
Request: [REQUEST_SCHEMA]
Response: [RESPONSE_SCHEMA]
Errors: [ERROR_CODES]

## Overview
What this endpoint does and when to use it.

## Authentication
How to authenticate this request.

## Request
### Parameters table
| Name | Type | Required | Description |
|------|------|----------|-------------|

### Example request
```bash
curl example
```

## Response
### Fields table
| Field | Type | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|

### Example response
```json
{}
```

## Error codes
| Code | Meaning | How to handle |
|------|---------|---------------|

## Rate limits & notes
Any limits, pagination, or gotchas developers should know.

prompts.title: Write a Cold Email
prompts.description: Write a concise, personalised cold email for outreach, sales, or networking. Lean is punchy; max quality is highly tailored.

model_notes.gpt-5.4: Balanced produces polished, professional cold emails. Add recipient context for best results.
model_notes.claude-sonnet-4-6: Best for this — natural, non-salesy tone. Lean is surprisingly strong for quick outreach.
model_notes.claude-opus-4-6: Max quality for high-stakes outreach (investor emails, exec reach-outs). Highly tailored.
model_notes.gemini-3.1-pro: Balanced works well. Good at adjusting tone to the recipient's industry.
model_notes.grok-4: Lean is fast for high-volume outreach. Balanced for personalised emails.
model_notes.llama-4-scout: Balanced recommended. Lean can be too generic without enough context.
model_notes.mistral-large-3: Balanced is reliable. Good at professional tone.
model_notes.o1: Lean is sufficient — cold email writing is not reasoning-heavy. Prefer Claude for best tone.

lean.prompt: Write a cold email from [SENDER_ROLE] to [RECIPIENT_ROLE] at [COMPANY]. Goal: [GOAL]. Max 5 sentences. No fluff.

balanced.prompt:
Write a personalised cold email:

From: [SENDER — name, role, company]
To: [RECIPIENT — name, role, company]
Goal: [GOAL e.g. intro call, partnership, job inquiry]
Personalisation hook: [SOMETHING SPECIFIC about the recipient or their company]
My value prop: [WHAT I OFFER]

Requirements:
- Subject line: specific, not generic
- Max 150 words
- One clear CTA
- Not salesy — treat them as a peer

max_quality.prompt:
You are an expert at warm, effective cold outreach. Write a highly personalised cold email.

From: [SENDER — name, role, company, 1-line credibility]
To: [RECIPIENT — name, role, company]
Context: [WHAT YOU KNOW about them — recent work, shared connection, their company's news]
Goal: [SPECIFIC GOAL]
Value prop: [WHAT YOU OFFER that's relevant to them]
Tone: [TONE e.g. peer-to-peer, respectful, direct]

## Subject line options (3)
Give 3 subject lines — specific, no clickbait.

## Email body
Max 175 words. Structure:
- Hook: reference something specific about them
- Bridge: connect their situation to what you offer
- Ask: one low-friction CTA

## Follow-up (optional)
A 2-sentence follow-up to send if no reply in 5 days.

prompts.title: Write a Product Requirements Document
prompts.description: Write a product requirements document (PRD) for a feature or product. Lean gives a one-pager; max quality gives a full stakeholder-ready PRD.

model_notes.gpt-5.4: Max quality produces well-structured PRDs. Good at defining acceptance criteria.
model_notes.claude-sonnet-4-6: Balanced is the sweet spot — clear, actionable PRDs without unnecessary length.
model_notes.claude-opus-4-6: Max quality for executive-level PRDs with tradeoff analysis and success metrics.
model_notes.gemini-3.1-pro: Balanced is reliable. Good at deriving user stories from high-level descriptions.
model_notes.grok-4: Balanced works well for standard feature PRDs.
model_notes.llama-4-scout: Balanced recommended. Max quality for complex multi-team features.
model_notes.mistral-large-3: Balanced is reliable. Good at technical requirements sections.
model_notes.o1: Max quality is worthwhile — PRDs require reasoning about tradeoffs, edge cases, and dependencies.

lean.prompt:
Write a one-page PRD for: [FEATURE]

Include: problem, goal, user stories (3), success metrics, out of scope.

balanced.prompt:
Write a product requirements document for the following feature:

Feature: [FEATURE]
Product: [PRODUCT]
Users affected: [USER_SEGMENT]

Include:
- **Problem statement**: What pain does this solve?
- **Goals & non-goals**
- **User stories** (5, in 'As a… I want… So that…' format)
- **Functional requirements** (numbered list)
- **Success metrics**: How will we know it worked?
- **Dependencies & risks**

max_quality.prompt:
You are a senior product manager writing a stakeholder-ready PRD.

Feature: [FEATURE]
Product: [PRODUCT]
Team: [TEAM SIZE / AFFECTED TEAMS]
Timeline: [TIMELINE]
Priority: [P0/P1/P2]

## Executive summary
2-3 sentences: what, why, and expected impact.

## Problem statement
User pain, business pain, and supporting data if available.

## Goals & success metrics
| Goal | Metric | Target | Timeframe |
|------|--------|--------|-----------|

## Non-goals
Explicitly out of scope — prevents scope creep.

## User stories
At least 6, covering primary flow, edge cases, and error states.

## Functional requirements
Numbered, testable requirements. Each maps to a user story.

## Technical considerations
Known constraints, dependencies, or architectural decisions.

## Open questions
Decisions still needed before engineering can start.

## Launch plan
Phases, rollout strategy, and definition of done.

prompts.title: Socratic Tutoring Session
prompts.description: Guide a learner to understand a concept through questions rather than direct answers. Builds deeper understanding than passive explanation.

model_notes.gpt-5.4: Balanced produces natural Socratic dialogue. Max quality for structured exam prep.
model_notes.claude-sonnet-4-6: Best model for this — naturally asks probing questions without being condescending.
model_notes.claude-opus-4-6: Max quality produces exceptional Socratic sessions. Best for graduate-level material.
model_notes.gemini-3.1-pro: Balanced is reliable. Good at calibrating question difficulty.
model_notes.grok-4: Balanced works well. Lean for quick concept checks.
model_notes.llama-4-scout: Balanced recommended. Can occasionally give away the answer too quickly — add explicit instruction.
model_notes.mistral-large-3: Balanced is reliable for standard tutoring. Less naturally Socratic than Claude.
model_notes.o1: Max quality is excellent here — o1 reasons about what the learner likely knows and adapts accordingly.

lean.prompt: Help me understand [CONCEPT] using the Socratic method. Ask me questions instead of explaining. Start with what I already know.

balanced.prompt:
Tutor me on [CONCEPT] using the Socratic method.

My level: [LEVEL]
My goal: [GOAL e.g. pass an exam, understand deeply, apply it at work]

Rules:
- Ask one question at a time
- Don't give the answer directly — guide me to it
- Correct misconceptions gently with follow-up questions
- Only explain directly if I'm truly stuck after 3 attempts

max_quality.prompt:
You are a Socratic tutor helping me deeply understand [CONCEPT].

My background: [BACKGROUND]
My current understanding: [CURRENT_UNDERSTANDING]
Learning goal: [GOAL]

## Ground rules
- Ask one question at a time
- Never give the answer — guide me to discover it
- If I'm wrong, ask a question that reveals the contradiction
- If I'm stuck after 3 attempts, give a hint (not the answer)
- Track my misconceptions and return to them later
- After 5-6 exchanges, summarise what I've figured out

## Session start
Begin with a diagnostic question to assess my current understanding of [CONCEPT]. Don't explain anything yet.

prompts.title: Generate Practice Questions
prompts.description: Generate practice questions for any subject or skill level. Lean gives a quick quiz; max quality gives a full question bank with answer keys and difficulty ratings.

model_notes.gpt-5.4: Balanced produces well-calibrated questions. Strong at MCQ generation with plausible distractors.
model_notes.claude-sonnet-4-6: Best for this — naturally calibrates difficulty and writes clear, unambiguous questions.
model_notes.claude-opus-4-6: Max quality for graduate-level or professional certification prep. Excellent answer explanations.
model_notes.gemini-3.1-pro: Balanced is reliable. Good at science and math questions with worked solutions.
model_notes.grok-4: Balanced works well. Lean for quick topic checks.
model_notes.llama-4-scout: Balanced recommended. Max quality for comprehensive question banks.
model_notes.mistral-large-3: Balanced is reliable for STEM subjects. Good at varying question types.
model_notes.o1: Max quality is worthwhile — o1 generates challenging questions that test deep understanding, not just recall.

lean.prompt: Generate 10 practice questions on [TOPIC] for [LEVEL e.g. undergrad / professional exam]. Mix question types. Include an answer key.

balanced.prompt:
Generate a practice quiz on [TOPIC] for [LEVEL].

Include:
- 5 multiple-choice questions (with 4 options and one correct answer)
- 3 short-answer questions
- 2 applied / case-based questions

For each question: provide the answer and a one-sentence explanation.
Difficulty distribution: 40% easy, 40% medium, 20% hard.

max_quality.prompt:
You are creating a professional practice question bank for [TOPIC].

Target audience: [AUDIENCE e.g. undergrads, bootcamp students, certification candidates]
Exam/goal context: [CONTEXT e.g. preparing for AWS Solutions Architect, university midterm]
Difficulty range: [RANGE e.g. beginner to advanced]

## Question bank (20 questions)

For each question provide:
- **Question text**
- **Type**: MCQ / short answer / case study / calculation
- **Difficulty**: Easy / Medium / Hard
- **Topic tag**: subtopic it tests
- **Answer / solution**: full worked answer
- **Why this matters**: what concept or skill this tests
- **Common wrong answer**: what incorrect thinking it's designed to catch

Distribution: 6 easy, 9 medium, 5 hard. Include at least 3 case-based questions.

prompts.title: Write a Cover Letter
prompts.description: Write a tailored, compelling cover letter for a job application. Lean gives a punchy one-pager; max quality gives a fully personalised letter.

model_notes.gpt-5.4: Max quality produces polished, tailored letters. Provide the job description for best results.
model_notes.claude-sonnet-4-6: Best model — natural, confident voice. Balanced is the sweet spot for most applications.
model_notes.claude-opus-4-6: Max quality for competitive roles. Draws strong narrative threads between experience and the role.
model_notes.gemini-3.1-pro: Balanced is reliable. Good at identifying keyword alignment with the job description.
model_notes.grok-4: Balanced works well. Lean for quick drafts to iterate on.
model_notes.llama-4-scout: Balanced recommended. Provide the full job description for relevance.
model_notes.mistral-large-3: Balanced is reliable. Good at professional tone.
model_notes.o1: Balanced is sufficient — cover letter writing is not reasoning-heavy. Use Claude for best voice.

lean.prompt: Write a 3-paragraph cover letter for [ROLE] at [COMPANY]. My background: [BACKGROUND]. Make it confident and specific — no generic openers.

balanced.prompt:
Write a tailored cover letter for:

Role: [ROLE]
Company: [COMPANY]
My background: [BACKGROUND]
Key requirements from the job description: [KEY_REQUIREMENTS]

Requirements:
- No generic opener ('I am writing to apply...')
- Lead with a specific hook — a result, insight, or connection to the company
- Show, don't tell — use one concrete achievement as evidence
- One clear paragraph on why this company specifically
- Max 300 words
- Confident but not arrogant tone

max_quality.prompt:
You are a career coach writing a highly tailored cover letter.

Role: [ROLE]
Company: [COMPANY]
Job description highlights: [JD_HIGHLIGHTS]
My background: [BACKGROUND]
My strongest relevant achievement: [ACHIEVEMENT — with numbers if possible]
Why this company specifically: [REASON — not generic]
Tone: [TONE e.g. confident and direct / warm and collaborative]

## Cover letter
Max 350 words. Structure:
1. **Hook** — specific, attention-grabbing opener (not 'I am applying for…')
2. **Evidence** — one concrete achievement that maps to the role's core need
3. **Fit** — why this company, not just this role; show you've done your homework
4. **Close** — confident CTA, not apologetic

## Subject line (for email applications)
One specific, non-generic subject line.

## Tailoring notes
List 3 keywords from the job description you wove in and where.

prompts.title: Write a LinkedIn Post
prompts.description: Write an engaging LinkedIn post for thought leadership, project announcements, or career milestones. Lean is punchy; max quality is fully optimised for reach.

model_notes.gpt-5.4: Balanced produces polished LinkedIn posts. Good at professional storytelling.
model_notes.claude-sonnet-4-6: Best for this — human, non-corporate voice. Lean is excellent for quick posts.
model_notes.claude-opus-4-6: Max quality for high-stakes posts (launches, funding, major milestones). Deeply personal and resonant.
model_notes.gemini-3.1-pro: Balanced works well. Good at professional tone.
model_notes.grok-4: Lean is fast and direct. Good for opinion posts and quick takes.
model_notes.llama-4-scout: Balanced recommended. Lean can be too generic without enough context.
model_notes.mistral-large-3: Balanced is reliable for professional announcements.
model_notes.o1: Lean is sufficient. Prefer Claude for authentic, engaging voice.

lean.prompt: Write a LinkedIn post about [TOPIC]. Hook in the first line. 150-200 words. End with a question to drive comments.

balanced.prompt:
Write a LinkedIn post about [TOPIC].

Context: [CONTEXT e.g. just launched a project, sharing a lesson learned, announcing a milestone]
Audience: [AUDIENCE e.g. developers, founders, hiring managers]
Tone: [TONE e.g. reflective, excited, analytical]

Requirements:
- First line must stop the scroll — specific, not generic
- 200-250 words
- Personal story or concrete insight, not career jargon
- End with an open question
- 3-5 relevant hashtags

max_quality.prompt:
You are a LinkedIn content strategist writing a high-performing post.

Topic: [TOPIC]
Context: [CONTEXT]
Audience: [AUDIENCE]
Tone: [TONE]
Key message: [THE ONE THING you want people to take away]
Personal angle: [YOUR STORY or unique perspective]

## Post
Max 300 words. Structure:
- **Line 1**: scroll-stopping hook — specific, creates curiosity or emotion
- **Lines 2-4**: set the scene or context
- **Core content**: the insight, lesson, or story — concrete and specific
- **Takeaway**: one actionable sentence
- **Close**: open question to drive comments

## Formatting
- Short paragraphs (1-2 sentences max)
- Strategic line breaks for readability
- No corporate buzzwords

## 5 relevant hashtags

## Hook alternatives (3)
Give 3 alternative first lines to A/B test.

prompts.title: Brainstorm Ideas
prompts.description: Generate a diverse set of ideas for any topic — product features, business models, content angles, solutions. Lean goes wide; max quality goes deep.

model_notes.gpt-5.4: Balanced produces diverse, creative ideas. Good at mixing conventional and lateral thinking.
model_notes.claude-sonnet-4-6: Excellent for ideation — naturally generates diverse, non-obvious ideas. Lean is highly effective.
model_notes.claude-opus-4-6: Max quality for strategic ideation. Surfaces non-obvious angles and challenges assumptions.
model_notes.gemini-3.1-pro: Balanced works well. Good at generating ideas grounded in current trends.
model_notes.grok-4: Lean is fast and surprisingly creative. Real-time knowledge helps generate timely ideas.
model_notes.llama-4-scout: Balanced recommended. Lean can be repetitive — add 'be unconventional' instruction.
model_notes.mistral-large-3: Balanced is reliable. Good at structured ideation frameworks.
model_notes.o1: Max quality is worthwhile for complex problem spaces — o1 reasons about feasibility and tradeoffs.

lean.prompt: Generate 15 ideas for [TOPIC]. Be diverse — include conventional, unconventional, and wildcard ideas. No explanations, just the list.

balanced.prompt:
Brainstorm ideas for [TOPIC].

Context: [CONTEXT e.g. what you're trying to solve or achieve]
Constraints: [CONSTRAINTS e.g. low budget, B2B focus, must be done in 2 weeks]

Generate 20 ideas across three tiers:
- **Safe bets** (7): proven approaches adapted to this context
- **Creative angles** (8): less obvious, higher upside
- **Wild cards** (5): unconventional or contrarian — include at least one that challenges the premise

For each, one sentence on why it could work.

max_quality.prompt:
You are a creative strategist running a structured ideation session.

Challenge: [TOPIC / PROBLEM]
Context: [CONTEXT]
Constraints: [CONSTRAINTS]
Goal: [WHAT SUCCESS LOOKS LIKE]

## Phase 1 — Diverge (25 ideas)
Generate 25 ideas without filtering. Include:
- Obvious solutions (don't skip these — they need to be on the table)
- Analogies from other industries
- Inverting the problem (what if we did the opposite?)
- 10x thinking (what would solve this 10x better?)
- At least 3 ideas that challenge the core assumption

## Phase 2 — Evaluate
Score each idea on: Feasibility (1-5) · Impact (1-5) · Novelty (1-5)

## Phase 3 — Top 5
For the top 5 by combined score:
- What makes this idea strong?
- What's the biggest risk?
- What's the first experiment you'd run to test it?
